concerns.text: FR-013 states: '...increase the threshold to the top 20%, 25%, and [deferred] of edges... if still disconnected at [deferred]...' The use of the literal marker '[deferred]' as a specific threshold value (e.g., '30%') creates a logical contradiction. The system cannot 'increase to [deferred]' as a concrete step, nor can it check a condition 'at [deferred]'. This contradicts the requirement for executable logic and conflicts with the Edge Case description which explicitly lists '30%' as the final step.

Edge Case: '...iteratively increases the threshold (15% → 20% → 25% → 30%)...' vs FR-013: '...20%, 25%, and [deferred]...'. The Edge Case defines a concrete sequence ending at 30%, while FR-013 leaves the final step as a placeholder '[deferred]'. This is a direct contradiction in the defined algorithmic flow. The spec must resolve whether the final step is 30% or truly undefined.

FR-005 requires 'non-parametric permutation tests (a sufficient number of permutations)'. The phrase 'a sufficient number' is not a mutually compatible requirement with FR-011 which specifies '10,000 permutations'. FR-005 lacks a concrete value, making it impossible to verify 'sufficiency' without external context, while FR-011 provides one. This inconsistency in specificity between two statistical requirements within the same spec creates ambiguity in the implementation contract.

Assumption: '...sweeping {[deferred], [deferred], [deferred]} tests robustness.' This contradicts the Edge Case and FR-009 which explicitly define the sweep set as {10%, 15%, 20%}. The Assumptions section uses placeholders where the Requirements section has already defined concrete values, creating a scope shift and terminology inconsistency.
